name: Astorga City Tour  — circular through the City
name_original: Astorga City Tour  — circular por la Ciudad
region_intro: Hiking around Astorga offers diverse trails through a region shaped by the Camino de Santiago and the Montes de León. The landscape features varied terrain, including oak forests, river valleys, and the distinctive Maragatería region. Hikers can explore sections of the historic pilgrimage route, experience the mountainous areas of the Montes de León, or discover the traditional architecture of local villages. The area provides a range of elevations, from gentle paths to more strenuous mountain ascents. Best hiking trails around Astorga The most popular hiking route is Astorga City Tour — circular through the City, a 6.9 miles (11.1 km) trail that takes 2 hours 53 minutes to complete. This route explores the city's historical and cultural sites. Another top favourite among local hikers is Murias de Rechivaldo – Santa Catalina de Somoza loop from Castrillo de los Polvazares, a moderate 10.0 miles (16.1 km) path. This trail winds through the traditional Maragatería region, showcasing its unique villages and surrounding countryside. Local hikers also love the Horse Head Door Knocker – El Ganso Village loop from Huerta Cimeras, a 4.3 miles (7.0 km) trail leading through rural landscapes and past small villages, often completed in about 1 hour 48 minutes. Hiking around Astorga is defined by its connection to the Camino de Santiago, the mountainous terrain of the Montes de León, and extensive oak forests. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easy walks to challenging mountain trails. The routes in Astorga are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 250 hikers have used komoot to explore Astorga's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes around Astorga?
A: Hiking around Astorga offers diverse terrain, from the relatively flat paths of the Camino de Santiago through farmlands and rustic villages, to the more challenging slopes of the Montes de León. You'll also find trails winding through extensive oak forests, river valleys, and the distinctive cultural landscapes of the Maragatería region.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or families in Astorga?
A: Yes, Astorga offers several easy trails perfect for beginners or families. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17541489" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Murals in Astorga – Pilgrim Monument loop from Astorga&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 3.8-mile (6.2 km) route. Another accessible option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38164084" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ecce Homo Hermitage – Murias de Rechivaldo loop from Astorga Cathedral&lt;/a&gt;, which is 4.3 miles (6.9 km) long and also rated easy.

Q: Can I find challenging hikes in the Astorga region?
A: Yes, for those seeking a challenge, the Astorga region provides access to the Montes de León, which feature steeper slopes and more strenuous experiences. While specific challenging routes are not detailed in the guide, the region's mountainous terrain offers opportunities for demanding ascents and longer treks.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while hiking near Astorga?
A: Hikers can explore a variety of natural features and landmarks. The region is known for its oak forests, river valleys, and the unique landscapes of the Maragatería. Notable landmarks include the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5898729" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cathedral of Saint Mary of Astorga&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4637297" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Episcopal Palace of Astorga (Gaudí Palace)&lt;/a&gt; within the city. Further afield, the Cruz de Ferro in the Montes de León is a significant pilgrim landmark offering panoramic views.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available around Astorga?
A: Yes, many routes around Astorga are circular. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1272356657/astorga-city-tour-circular-through-the-city" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Astorga City Tour — circular through the City&lt;/a&gt;, which is 6.9 miles (11.1 km) long. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38164803" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Murias de Rechivaldo – Santa Catalina de Somoza loop from Castrillo de los Polvazares&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 10.0-mile (16.1 km) path through the Maragatería region.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Astorga?
A: The best time for hiking in Astorga is generally during spring and autumn when the weather is mild and comfortable. Summer can be warm, especially in open areas, while winter may bring colder temperatures and potentially snow in the higher elevations of the Montes de León.

Q: Are there any hikes that pass through traditional villages or cultural sites?
A: Absolutely. The region is rich in cultural heritage. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38164803" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Murias de Rechivaldo – Santa Catalina de Somoza loop from Castrillo de los Polvazares&lt;/a&gt; takes you through the traditional Maragatería region, showcasing its unique villages and architecture. The Camino de Santiago itself also passes through many rustic villages.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Astorga?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 50 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, the historical significance of the Camino de Santiago, and the variety of trails catering to different fitness levels.

Q: Are there any hikes that feature waterfalls or river views?
A: Yes, the region offers routes with water features. The Cañones del Górgora Trail, near Monteal, is known for its waterfalls and canyon scrambling sections. Other trails, like the El Celemín Route, offer views of the Porcos valley and reservoirs, providing scenic riverine experiences.

Q: Can I access hiking trails around Astorga using public transport?
A: Astorga is a key stop on the Camino de Santiago, which means some sections of the pilgrimage route are accessible by public transport connections to the city. For specific trailheads further afield, local bus services to surrounding villages might be available, but it's advisable to check schedules in advance.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly hiking options near Astorga?
A: Many of the natural trails and paths around Astorga are suitable for hiking with dogs, provided they are kept under control. Always check local regulations for specific areas, especially within protected natural spaces or private lands, and ensure your dog is well-behaved around other hikers and livestock.

Q: Where can I find parking for hiking trails around Astorga?
A: For trails starting directly in Astorga, parking can typically be found within the city, often near the historic center or residential areas. For routes beginning in smaller villages, parking is usually available in village squares or designated areas. It's recommended to check specific trailhead information for the best parking options.
tips: Gaudí's episcopal palace, in Astorga, is a neo-Gothic style building designed by the modernist architect Antonio Gaudí. Due to its uniqueness, it constitutes one of the most outstanding buildings in Astorga's monumental heritage. In 1963 it became the Caminos Museum, and numerous and outstanding pieces from the Diocese of Astorga are exhibited, including prehistory, epigraphy and Roman numismatics, religious art from various periods and contemporary art.

It occupies the site that according to tradition was the house of Santa Marta, patron saint of Astorga. There are reasons to believe that there was one of the many monasteries that Astorga had in the Middle Ages.

Its construction began in 1683 with traces of the master stonemason Francisco de la Lastra. The towers bear the coat of arms of the Marquis of Astorga together with the city's coat of arms, and the façade is crowned with gargoyles, sculptures and balustrades.

Hermitage located at the exit of Astorga.
Mandatory stop on the way to Santiago. Built in the 16th century.

waypoints.name: Cathedral of Saint Mary of Astorga
